fr:mainmatter.html:p: Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Algorithm. For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

Remark. Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

Remark. \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time.

This was basically a historical account of the history of deep learning. The main focus was championing the likes of Hinton, Lecun, and Bengio with their unwavering conviction toward deep learning. Honestly, this book has made me excited and a bit intimidated about research. I do not know if I have the same obsessive commitment to my work as Hinton, etc. Also, for future reference, I feel like I will get a lot more out of books I am not already so familiar with. Technically, I thought it did an OK job, but I once again do not really enjoy reading books on my field for a broad audience. Learning about the industry origins i.e. DNN, Deepmind acquisition, etc. was pretty interesting though.
Enjoyable read. 5/10.

Overall, I thoroughly enjoyed this book. As a reinforcement learning afficianado, I have a newfound fandom for the mission of Deepmind. In particular, I was at the RLC talk given by David Silver mentioned in the book. However, I must acknowledge the deep bias in favor of Demis and Deepmind in the LLM race towards the end. It is easy to come away from this, thinking that Sam Altman is some sort of Silicon Valley super villain and Demis is our champion of humanity.
If I had to give an unbiased rating, I would give this book a 6.5/10. However, as a biased reader, I give it an 8/10 :)

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Due to the unstructured nature of PhD life / knowledge work in general, I have been trying to come up with the most effective system to ensure I am Getting Things Done (ironically I found this system actually not that helpful).

Since Cal Newport is a professor (can relate to the knowledge work problem), I have been reading a lot of his stuff on productivity. I read Deep Work awhile back and the guiding principles make sense to me. However, I still struggle to actually implement it.
I often refer back to Atomic Habits as it intuitively just makes so much sense to me.

html:ul.html:li: Start small: starting a habit should take less than 2 minutes.
Never miss a habit twice.
Habit anchors: I am always going to run so now after I run I stretch, which has resulted in remarkable stretching consistency (see my Weeknotes).
Habit tracking: Something I am also implementing in my Weeknotes with habitify.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:

html:ol.html:li: Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is effectively a task management system.

Work streams. I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:

html:ul.html:li: Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:

html:ol.html:li: Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next

html:ul.html:li: An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.

html:ol.html:li: A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.

html:ul.html:li: For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,

html:ol.html:li: Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).

html:ul.html:li: Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.

html:ol.html:li: Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.

html:ul.html:li: Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.

html:ul.html:li: I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.

I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:

html:ol.html:li: Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is effectively a task management system.

Work streams. I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:

html:ul.html:li: Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:

html:ol.html:li: Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next

html:ul.html:li: An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.

html:ol.html:li: A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.

html:ul.html:li: For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,

html:ol.html:li: Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).

html:ul.html:li: Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.

html:ol.html:li: Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.

html:ul.html:li: Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.

html:ul.html:li: I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.

fr:title.text: Putting the engineer in my engineering degree

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Putting the engineer in my engineering degree

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here I will be learning not only how a computer actually works, but how to actually leverage this knowledge to write more performant code. Along the way, I also intend to learn good coding practice and become all around a more capable engineer to differentiate myself from the hardware unaware.
I will also be keeping track of miscellaneous notes here that I deem relevant and things I want to look at in the future.

Physical memory is finite, so the OS reserves a region of disk — swap space — to hold pages that don't fit. Pages currently on disk have their present bit cleared in the Page table entry; the PTE instead holds a disk address.
When a process touches a virtual address whose page is not present, the hardware traps to the OS. This is a page fault. The handler:

html:ol.html:li: Looks at the PTE: if invalid, the access is illegal — terminate the process.
If valid but not present: read the page in from swap, update the PTE, retry the access.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware never handles page faults itself — that would require it to understand disk I/O and the OS's swap layout. Page-fault handling is always in software.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The three cases on every memory access:

html:ol.html:li: Present + valid: hardware reads the PPN from the PTE and proceeds.
Not present + valid: hardware invokes the page-fault handler to retrieve from swap.
Not valid: hardware raises an exception; the OS typically kills the process.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. What happens when you dereference a nullptr? The TLB misses on VPN 0, the page-table walk finds the PTE marked invalid, and the hardware traps to the OS — which delivers SIGSEGV.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often hear this question come up amongst performance-driven developers, and for the longest time I could never get a straight answer as to why the stack was so much better? After a long foray into the inner workings of how memory allocation is actually done, I think I finally have an answer. I do not want to forget this, so I am doing a quick write up here.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This all comes down to what happens during each memory allocation. Prior to actually running the program, the operating system allocates some amount of memory for both the stack and the heap (I think around 2-4MB?). When you allocate memory on the stack i.e. int x = 1;
Stack just increments stack pointer.
Writes value to new address pointed to by stack pointer. When you allocate memory on the heap i.e. int *x = malloc(sizeof(int)), we must instead choose how best to fulfill the request, which is a whole research direction in itself.
One such implementation is the buddy algorithm However, malloc itself is not a system call and therefore is not terribly slow. A real issue occurs when we run out of pre-allocated heap memory. This requires invoking a system call like sbrk to increment the size of the allocated memory for the heap.
If no available pages, OS must evict and write to disk...
Formally, this is known as "heap growth". Generally, if you know how much memory you need it is good practice to just allocated all up front to minimize any potential system calls. With this, the performance difference is not too drastic :)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I used LazyVim for a long time, but I kept hitting a wall: understanding how LazyVim did something was consistently harder than if I had just written the thing myself. So I gave myself one day to rebuild from scratch, with the modest goal of first-class support for \TeX , Python, Forester, and C/C++. That one-day sprint is long past, and the config has kept evolving since — this note describes where it landed, not the frantic first pass. The through-line is that almost everything now leans on Neovim's own primitives instead of a framework, which is exactly the transparency I was missing.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The whole config lives in my dotfiles. It is a thin init.lua that loads four modules — config.lazy, config.options, config.keymaps, and core.lsp — with the actual plugin specs under lua/plugins/ and per-server LSP tables under lsp/.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To my surprise, a lot of the behavior I had taken for granted turned out to be options LazyVim set internally. I was greeted on first launch by 8-space indents and a clipboard that would not survive a trip between terminal instances. So the first job was archaeology: I dug through LazyVim for every option I actually liked and copied them into config/options.lua. System clipboard, sensible folding via Treesitter (foldexpr = v:lua.vim.treesitter.foldexpr()), a global statusline, conceallevel for markup — the unglamorous foundation everything else sits on.

The plugin manager is lazy.nvim, the de facto standard, and it genuinely just works. The bootstrap snippet from their docs clones it on first run, and after that every file under lua/plugins/ that returns a spec table is imported automatically. No central manifest to keep in sync — a new file is a new plugin.

This is where the config diverged most sharply from where it started. The first pass used mason.nvim, mason-lspconfig, and nvim-lspconfig — the standard stack. All three are now gone. Neovim 0.11 ships a native LSP configuration API: vim.lsp.config defines a server and vim.lsp.enable turns it on, and any table returned from lsp/<name>.lua on the runtime path is picked up as that server's config. So core.lsp is just a list —

— and each server gets a plain Lua file in lsp/. No package manager, no bridge plugin translating between two config schemas. I install the servers themselves through my system package manager and let Neovim wire them up. It is dramatically less machinery for the same result, and when something breaks there is exactly one place to look.

The Python setup is the clearest upgrade. I dropped pyright for basedpyright and split responsibilities: basedpyright does type checking and inlay hints, while ruff runs as its own language server handling import organization and diagnostics. In the LazyVim days I fought constantly with import resolution, hand-writing a function to locate each project's virtualenv and pin pythonPath to its interpreter. basedpyright resolves environments well enough on its own that the hack is gone. clangd, texlab, and lua_ls round out the set, each configured in its own file — texlab in particular carries a fair amount of custom command wiring for building, forward search into Zathura, and environment manipulation.

A shared LspAttach autocommand in core.lsp binds the buffer-local LSP keymaps once, for every server: go-to-definition and friends route through fzf-lua pickers, and document highlighting, inlay-hint toggling, and native completion get enabled per-buffer based on what each client actually supports.

fr:title.text: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The original post described an ugly hack to get Forester completion. The forester.nvim plugin exposed a completion source, but only for nvim-cmp; blink.cmp (which I use — see below) needs a blink.compat shim, and that shim expected a plugin to be a source rather than to bury one inside a larger feature set. Getting reference completion working meant reading blink.compat internals and registering the source by hand.

None of that survives, because Forester now ships an actual language server. My lsp/forester.lua just launches forester lsp and hands it the forest root. The one wrinkle worth recording: forester locates the forest by its process working directory and ignores the LSP rootUri, and Neovim does not spawn the server in root_dir by default. So the config resolves the nearest forest.toml and starts the RPC process with an explicit cwd — otherwise the server exits with status 1 whenever Neovim's cwd has no forest.toml (e.g. when I open a tree from $HOME). Reference completion — indispensable for writing this very blog — now flows through the standard LSP path with no shim in sight.

Completion is blink.cmp, the successor that finally displaced nvim-cmp. Its sources are lsp, path, snippets, and omni, with LuaSnip as the snippet engine. Because I lean on native LSP, the on_attach handler calls vim.lsp.completion.enable with autotrigger, so the server's completions feed the picker directly.

Layered on top is supermaven-nvim for AI ghost-text suggestions — accept-suggestion, accept-word, and clear bound to <C-l>, <C-j>, and <C-]>. It fills the role Copilot-style tools play elsewhere without getting in the way of the structured LSP and snippet completions.

Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always wanted snippets that auto-expand. Under LazyVim I had settled for friendly-snippets on native Neovim snippets; rebuilding from scratch, I refused to compromise. The engine is LuaSnip, which blink.cmp supports natively, and I have to plug the luasnip-latex-snippets repo that ports the original UltiSnips math snippets over. My own snippets live in a small local plugin, custom-luasnip-snippets.nvim, vendored under lua/local/.

The trick I had wanted for years and can finally do: load LaTeX math snippets while inside math environments in Forester. The snippet plugin runs with use_treesitter = true, so it inspects the Forester Treesitter grammar, finds the node types for math environments, and only expands the math snippets when the cursor is actually inside one. Writing \TeX inside a .tree file feels exactly like writing it in a .tex file.

Formatting runs through conform.nvim on save. Python is handled by ruff_format followed by pyink, Lua by stylua — this replaced the old black + isort pairing, folding most of it into the same ruff that already runs as a language server. Formatting falls back to the LSP formatter when no dedicated tool is configured for a filetype.

The fuzzy finder is fzf-lua, which replaced Telescope. File and grep pickers are scoped to a detected project root — a small utils.root module (lifted from LazyVim) walks LSP workspace folders, .git, and cwd to find it — with a dedicated tex override that restricts live-grep to \TeX source files.

The piece I am most fond of is forester-new, a local plugin that wires the editor into the forest's own note pipeline. It shells out to scripts/new so every tree gets the standard frontmatter and routing, then commands like :ForesterNew, :ForesterStub, :ForesterCitek, and :ForesterTransient handle the follow-through: creating a tree and inserting a \transclude, stubbing a concept and dropping a [[id]] link at the cursor, or — in visual mode — lifting a selection into a new tree and replacing it in place with the transclude. Stub-and-link without ever leaving the sentence, which is exactly the friction the forest workflow is supposed to remove.

I originally built a whole in-editor Anki workflow on top of AnkiConnect and a vendored anki.nvim. That has since moved out of the editor and into the forest itself: cards are just \taxon{Card} trees, and scripts/anki-sync mirrors them into Anki — creating the note on first sync and updating in place thereafter. Writing a card is now the same as writing any other tree, snippets and all, and the sync is one command away. This is an example of a card.

Setup. We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
Answer. Yes!!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation. Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?
Answer. The reparametrization trick!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

For q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _{\phi }(x)). The reparametrization trick allows us to differentate through a random sample by sampling from q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) via z(\phi ) = \mu _{\phi }(x) + \sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon , where \epsilon \sim N(0, 1). Then, for a given sample \epsilon , we can easily compute 
abla _{\phi } z(\phi ) = 
abla _{\phi } \mu _{\phi }(x) + 
abla _{\phi }\sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As I grow more interested in abandoning batched updates and the replay buffer, the importance of elibility traces grows with me. Due to this, I thought it finally time to re-prove / understand what makes these traces so eligible.
The reference followed is Sutton and Barto (Ch 12.).
Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Algorithm. For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

Remark. Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

Remark. \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.
